questions.prompt: You are given a table orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total). Product team asks for a quick list of order_id and total for all orders placed on 2025-01-15. Which SELECT projects only needed columns and filters by date? Use the table.
options: SELECT order_id, total FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2025-01-15';
SELECT order_id, total, customer_id FROM orders;
SELECT DISTINCT total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-15' AND '2025-01-15';
questions.expected: SELECT order_id, total FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2025-01-15';

questions.prompt: Team lead asks: to find customers with total order value > 1000, you can write WHERE total > 1000 on the orders table and get per-customer results. Based on the orders(order_id, customer_id, total) table, this single WHERE will correctly list customers meeting that aggregate threshold.

Using orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total), write a short SQL query that returns each customer_id and their total spent across all orders, but only include customers whose total spent is at least 500. Use GROUP BY and an aggregate.
questions.expected: SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(total) >= 500;
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(total) >= 500;

questions.prompt: The product database has customers(customer_id, name) and orders(order_id, customer_id, total). You need every customer and their latest order total if any; customers without orders should still appear with NULL. Which JOIN and query accomplishes this correctly?
options: SELECT c.customer_id, o.total FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;
SELECT c.customer_id, MAX(o.total) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id;
SELECT c.customer_id, o.total FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id AND o.order_date = (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders o2 WHERE o2.customer_id = c.customer_id);
SELECT o.customer_id, o.total FROM orders o RIGHT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.order_date = (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders WHERE customer_id = o.customer_id);
questions.expected: SELECT c.customer_id, o.total FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id AND o.order_date = (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders o2 WHERE o2.customer_id = c.customer_id);

questions.prompt: Analytics asks for a running total of daily sales ordered by order_date for 2025-01- (first 20 days). Given orders(order_id, order_date, total), which SQL using a window function produces a cumulative sum per day (assume one row per order)?
options: SELECT order_date, SUM(total) FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-20' GROUP BY order_date ORDER BY order_date;
SELECT order_date, total, SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-20' ORDER BY order_date;
SELECT order_date, total, SUM(total) AS running_total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-20' GROUP BY order_date ORDER BY order_date;
SELECT order_date, total, SUM(total) OVER (PARTITION BY order_date) AS running_total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-20';
questions.expected: SELECT order_date, total, SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_total FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-20' ORDER BY order_date;

questions.prompt: Your team considers denormalizing a product_orders table by storing customer_address on every order row to speed reads for reporting. Describe tradeoffs and recommend whether to denormalize in a high-write, read-heavy reporting system. Use at least two pieces of evidence: expected update frequency of addresses and read latency requirements.
questions.idealAnswer: Recommendation: avoid denormalizing if addresses change frequently or strong consistency is required; instead use caching or materialized views. Evidence 1: addresses update ~weekly -> consistency cost high. Evidence 2: reporting SLA requires <200ms reads -> consider read replica and indexed joins or cached materialized view to meet latency with normalized schema.

questions.prompt: A slow query joins orders and order_items; EXPLAIN shows a sequential scan on order_items and nested loop joining orders on item_id, causing high latency. Based on this evidence, diagnose the primary performance issues and propose two concrete fixes mentioning how each addresses the shown plan.
questions.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: missing index on order_items.item_id causing seq scan; nested loop indicates many iterations. Fix 1: add index on order_items.item_id to enable index scan and reduce scanned rows. Fix 2: rewrite join to use hash join by ensuring sufficient memory or force planner with optimizer hints or aggregate beforehand to reduce rows.

questions.prompt: Write the logical steps (sequence) you would use to build a single SQL query that returns top 5 customers by total spending in 2025, including customer name and total_spent. Steps should form an ordered pipeline from raw tables to final result. Use customers(customer_id,name) and orders(order_id,customer_id,order_date,total).
sequencePool: GROUP BY customer_id and compute SUM(total) AS total_spent
JOIN the aggregated results to customers ON customer_id to get customer name
Filter orders to order_date between '2025-01-01' and '2025-12-31'
expected: Filter orders to order_date between '2025-01-01' and '2025-12-31'
GROUP BY customer_id and compute SUM(total) AS total_spent
JOIN the aggregated results to customers ON customer_id to get customer name

questions.prompt: You must ensure reports run with consistent snapshot semantics while multiple updates occur. Which transaction isolation level should you choose to prevent non-repeatable reads and phantom reads for analytic reports, and why? Provide the level name and a one-sentence justification referencing snapshot behavior.
questions.expected: SERIALIZABLE — it ensures a serializable snapshot preventing both non-repeatable reads and phantoms by executing transactions as if sequentially.
questions.idealAnswer: SERIALIZABLE — it ensures a serializable snapshot preventing both non-repeatable reads and phantoms by executing transactions as if sequentially.

questions.answer: SELECT o.customer_id, o.total FROM orders o RIGHT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.order_date = (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM orders WHERE customer_id = o.customer_id);
